content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive-Legal · · Enforcement · · Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5660          322-3901          322-6641          322-6444

April 1, 1981

Theodore G. Morris
City Attorney
City of Pacific Grove
300 Forest Avenue
Pacific Grove, CA 93950

Re: Your letter of March 20, 1981

Dear Mr. Morris:

Thank you for your letter dated March 20, 1981, requesting
advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission regarding
the potential conflict of interest of one of your Planning
Commissioners. You have stated that the Commissioner owns
more than $10,000 in a limited partnership agreement with a
developer in your city. In addition, another such arrangement
exists between them outside of your city. In an unrelated
development, the developer seeks action by the Planning
Commission. The Commissioner has been charged with a conflict
but feels he has none. However, before the Fair Political
Practices Commission can provide written advice, additional
information will be necessary.1/

In the meantime, perhaps this informal advice will be
of assistance to you in advising your client. Government
Code Section 87100-2/ provides:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

(Emphasis added.)

1/    Section 83114(b) requires that the Commission have
before it "all the material facts," including the individual's
name.

2/    Unless otherwise specified, all references will be
to the Government Code.

Theodore G. Morris
April 1, 1981
Page Two


     Thus, if it is determined that the requisite financial
interest exists, then the Planning Commissioner would be
precluded from taking part in the decision and from appearing
"as an unpaid advocate at the developer's application at a
hearing before his own commission...."2/

     Section 87103 defines financial interest.  This is
further detailed in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18702 and
18703.

     Should you or your client desire specific written advice,
we would request the following information:  (1) Copy of the
newspaper story referred to in your letter; (2) copies of
the actual partnership agreements involving the developer
and Planning Commissioner; 3/ (3) whether income is being
received by the properties owned by these partnership
arrangements, and, if so, whether more than $250 has been
received by the Planning Commissioner; (4) how proximate the
project in question is to the condominium project in Pacific
Grove in which the Planning Commissioner is a partner; (5)
the Planning Commissioner's name; and (6) any other material
facts not specified above.

     It is hoped that this information will be helpful to
you.  If you have any additional questions, please contact
the undersigned.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:ph

_____________

2/    See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 for further
clarification of the terminology of Section 87103.

3/    You have forwarded a copy of a "typical agreement"
but not the one actually involved.

CITY COUNCIL
FLORIS O. WILLIAMS
MAYOR
JAMES H. HUGHES, D.D.S
MORRIS G. FISHER
MADELYN A. SLOAN
IRIS FRANCO
DON MARTINE
DAVID J. EATON

GARY W. BALES
CITY MANAGER
WILLIAM S. PITT
ADMIN. SERVICES DIRECTOR
CITY CLERK AND TREASURER
THEODORE G. MORRIS
CITY ATTORNEY

CITY OF PACIFIC GROVE

300 FOREST AVENUE
PACIFIC GROVE, CALIFORNIA 93950
TELEPHONE (408) 375-3861

March 20, 1981

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Attn: Diane Fishburn

Dear Ms. Fishburn:

I request an opinion as to whether or not a conflict of interest exists in
respect of a Planning Commission sitting in judgment on an application to convert
a 9 acre mobilhome park into condominium residential development, where the
Planning Commissioner is a limited partner with the developer in another condo-
minimum development in the City.

Enclosed is the typical form of Limited Partnership Agreement used by this
developer, indicated as "General Partner" thereon, for a project outside of our
City.

The Commissioner in question has indicated on his disclosure statement that
his interest is more than $10,000 in this other project within the City. Additional-
ly, he has acknowledged that he made a similar form of investment with the same
developer in the neighboring City of Monterey.

The Commissioner in question insists that he has no conflict of interest.
Although he abstained and absented himself from the first consideration of this
project (because of newspaper reports of his relationship), it is conceivable that
he would again sit in judgment on some phase of the proposed development. Or, as
he suggested to me, he might appear as an unpaid advocate of the developer's applica-
tion at a hearing before his own commission or before the City Council.

Thus, I really have two questions:

(1) Does membership as a limited partner in the enclosed form of Agreement
create a conflict of interest in judging an application by the same
General Partner for a project in which the Planning Commissioner has
no interest?

(2) If such membership does not create a conflict, can the Commissioner appear
as an advocate of the developer's application before the Planning Commission?

Please call me if further particulars are needed.

Very truly yours,

[signed]
THEODORE G. MORRIS
City Attorney

TGM/bjv
